content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 29 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 115v–115r | **Source Images:** DEU_029_page_001.jpg; DEU_029_page_002.jpg; DEU_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg; DEU_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** These are the terms of the covenant the LORD commanded Moses to make with the Israelites in Moab, in addition to the covenant he had made with them at Horeb.

**2** Moses summoned all the Israelites and said to them:

"Your own eyes have seen everything the LORD did in Egypt to Pharaoh, to all his officials, and to all his land. **3** With your own eyes you saw those great trials, those signs and great wonders. **4** But to this day the LORD has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear.

**5** "Yet the LORD says: During the forty years I led you through the wilderness, your clothes did not wear out, nor did the sandals on your feet. **6** You ate no bread and drank no wine or other fermented drink. I did this so you would know that I am the LORD your God.

**7** "When you reached this place, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan came out to fight against us, but we defeated them. **8** We took their land and gave it as an inheritance to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.

**9** "Carefully follow the terms of this covenant, so that you may prosper in everything you do. **10** All of you are standing today in the presence of the LORD your God — your leaders and chief men, your elders and officials, and all the other men of Israel, **11** together with your children and your wives, and the foreigners living in your camps who chop your wood and carry your water. **12** You are standing here in order to enter into a covenant with the LORD your God, a covenant the LORD is making with you this day and sealing with an oath, **13** to confirm you this day as his people, that he may be your God as he promised you and as he swore to your ancestors, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

**14** "I am making this covenant, with its oath, not only with you **15** who are standing here with us today in the presence of the LORD our God but also with those who are not here today.

**16** "You yourselves know how we lived in Egypt and how we passed through the countries on the way here. **17** You saw among them their detestable images and idols of wood and stone, of silver and gold. **18** Make sure there is no man or woman, clan or tribe among you today whose heart turns away from the LORD our God to go and worship the gods of those nations; make sure there is no root among you that produces such bitter poison.

**19** "When such a person hears the words of this oath and they invoke a blessing on themselves, thinking, 'I will be safe, even though I persist in going my own stubborn way' — this will bring disaster on the well-watered land as well as the dry. **20** The LORD will never be willing to forgive them; his wrath and zeal will burn against them. All the curses written in this book will fall on them, and the LORD will blot out their name from under heaven. **21** The LORD will single them out from all the tribes of Israel for disaster, according to all the curses of the covenant written in this Book of the Instruction.

**22** "Your children who follow you in later generations and foreigners who come from distant lands will see the calamities that have fallen on the land and the diseases with which the LORD has afflicted it. **23** The whole land will be a burning waste of salt and sulfur — nothing planted, nothing sprouting, no vegetation growing on it. It will be like the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboyim, which the LORD overthrew in fierce anger. **24** All the nations will ask: 'Why has the LORD done this to this land? Why this fierce, burning anger?'

**25** "And the answer will be: 'It is because this people abandoned the covenant of the LORD, the God of their ancestors, the covenant he made with them when he brought them out of Egypt. **26** They went off and worshiped other gods and bowed down to them — gods they did not know, gods he had not given them. **27** Therefore the LORD's anger burned against this land, so that he brought on it all the curses written in this book. **28** In furious anger and in great wrath the LORD uprooted them from their land and thrust them into another land, as it is now.'

**29** "The hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever, that we may follow all the words of this instruction."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — Chapter placement note:** In the Hebrew Bible (and many manuscripts), this verse is the closing of chapter 28 or a heading for the Moab covenant section; English versification treats it as 29:1. PNEUMA follows the English verse numbering as given.

**Verse 4 — Eyes that do not see:** The striking statement that despite witnessing all the plagues, Israel still lacks a lev mevin ("understanding heart"), seeing eyes, and hearing ears is a profound theological confession. PNEUMA renders lev as "mind" here (it is the seat of thought and will) rather than simply "heart," because the failure being described is one of comprehension and response, not affection alone.

**Verses 5-6 — Miraculous preservation in the wilderness:** The divine care during the wandering is stated matter-of-factly — clothes and sandals that never wore out, supernatural sustenance without bread or wine. PNEUMA keeps the tone declarative ("I did this so you would know") to reflect the LORD's own voice here, embedded within Moses' speech.

**Verses 10-11 — The full community:** The covenant assembly is remarkable in its scope: leaders, elders, officials, all men, children, wives, and the foreigners (gerim) who do the most menial labor — woodcutters and water-carriers. PNEUMA renders this in full because the explicit inclusion of the lowest-status persons in the covenant community is theologically significant. There is no one outside the covenant circle.

**Verses 14-15 — Those not present:** The covenant extends beyond the living assembly to those not yet born and to future generations. This is the theological basis for Jewish understanding of covenant solidarity across time. PNEUMA renders "those who are not here today" to keep the temporal openness.

**Verse 18 — "Root that produces bitter poison":** The Hebrew shoresh poreh rosh ve-la'anah ("root producing poison and wormwood") is a mixed metaphor — the secret idolater is like a toxic plant hidden underground. PNEUMA renders it as "root among you that produces such bitter poison" to preserve the agricultural-toxicological image.

**Verse 19 — The self-deceived person:** The phrase "I will be safe, even though I persist in going my own stubborn way" renders the Hebrew be-sheririut libi elekh — literally "in the stubbornness of my heart I will walk." This is the dangerous inner voice of covenant presumptuousness, someone who assumes the blessing applies to them regardless of their loyalty. PNEUMA renders this as a first-person self-blessing, because the Hebrew has this person invoking blessing on themselves (hitbarekh bilvavo).

**Verse 23 — Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboyim:** All four cities of the plain are named. PNEUMA retains all four, as their combined desolation is the benchmark for total divine judgment on the land.

**Verse 29 — The great divide:** Ha-nistarot la-YHWH Eloheinu ve-ha-niglot lanu u-livaneinu — "The hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children." This is one of the most quoted verses in Deuteronomy. PNEUMA renders it with maximum clarity: God retains the mysteries; the community is responsible only for what has been disclosed. It is simultaneously a limit on human curiosity and a sufficient mandate for covenant living.

— PNEUMA
